options.text: Hey, you dropped the bullet point format. Can you redo section 4 with bullet points?

You've drifted from my formatting rules. Let me restate all of my original instructions: (1) use bullet points, not paragraphs, (2) keep each section under 100 words, (3) cite all sources with links. Please redo section 4 following all three rules, and apply them to every section going forward.

responses.response_weak:
I apologize for the error! Here's a revised version of section 4:

The impact of social media on political discourse has been extensively studied in recent years. Researchers have found that platform algorithms tend to amplify emotionally charged content, which can lead to increased polarization among users. Several studies have noted that echo chambers form naturally as users engage primarily with content that confirms their existing views, creating feedback loops that reinforce rather than challenge beliefs. This dynamic has raised significant concerns about the long-term effects on democratic processes and informed public debate.
responses.response_medium:
Sure, here's section 4 reformatted with bullet points:

- Social media algorithms amplify emotionally charged content, contributing to political polarization across demographic groups
- Echo chambers form naturally as users engage primarily with content that confirms their existing beliefs, creating self-reinforcing feedback loops
- Platform design choices like engagement-based ranking reward divisive posts over nuanced discussion
- Research suggests that exposure to opposing views on social media can paradoxically increase polarization rather than reduce it
- Younger users may be particularly susceptible to algorithm-driven content curation and its polarizing effects on their worldview
responses.response_strong:
You're right — I drifted from your rules. Here's section 4, corrected to follow all three:

**Section 4: Social Media and Political Discourse**

- Algorithms amplify emotionally charged content, increasing polarization ([Bail et al., 2018](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1804840115))
- Echo chambers form through engagement-based ranking ([Pariser, *The Filter Bubble*, 2011](https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/309214/the-filter-bubble-by-eli-pariser/))
- Exposure to opposing views may backfire, hardening existing positions ([NYU Social Media Lab](https://csmapnyu.org/))

Word count: 48 ✓ | Bullet points ✓ | Sources cited ✓

I'll apply all three rules — bullets, under 100 words, cited sources — to every section from here forward. Want me to also review sections 1–3 for consistency?

feedback.what_happened: The weak prompt says 'fix it' without specifying what's wrong, so the AI has no idea which rules it broke — the revised section comes back still in paragraphs, still without citations, and still over 100 words. The medium prompt correctly identifies the missing bullet points, and the AI fixes that one issue — but the section is still too long and has no source citations, because those rules weren't mentioned. The effective prompt restates all three original rules together, so the AI fixes everything at once and confirms it will follow all the rules going forward.
feedback.skill_name: When AI drifts from your instructions in a long conversation, restate ALL of your rules — not just the one you noticed. AI doesn't remember your earlier instructions the way you do.
feedback.tip: When you notice AI dropped one of your requirements, don't just fix that one thing. Restate ALL your original instructions in a single message — AI tends to lose multiple rules at once, not just the one you caught.
